meta.title: Last-Minute Late-October Backpacking in the US
meta.why_late_october: High country (Sierra, Rockies, Cascades, northern Appalachians) is already getting snow and hard freezes, so the sweet spot shifts to the desert Southwest, southern California, mild coastlines, and the southern/Appalachian foliage belt where the brutal summer heat has finally broken. Daylight is ~11 hours; pack a warm bag because desert and canyon nights can drop near or below freezing even when afternoons are mild.

bookability_legend.open_access: No reservation needed — self-register at a trailhead board or get a free walk-up permit. True 'decide on a whim' options.
bookability_legend.easy_now: Reservations available right now (recreation.gov, park system, or a ferry/site booking); rarely sells out for late October.
bookability_legend.competitive: Bookable but contested; apply now and have backup dates / last-minute-drawing or waitlist options.
bookability_legend.unavailable: Effectively impossible to secure last-minute for late October.

meta.note: Permit rules, quotas, and seasonal/fire closures change. Always confirm on the official site for each destination before committing. Some higher-elevation entries (e.g., Bryce) carry real early-snow risk in late October — see each destination's cons.

destinations.name: Grand Canyon National Park (Corridor)
destinations.bookability_note: Rolling backcountry permit system; requests for October open ~4 months ahead. Corridor is contested; less-traveled routes have more room. Walk-up waitlist exists.
destinations.best_route: South Kaibab down / Bright Angel up, overnight at Bright Angel Campground (rim-to-river)
destinations.terrain: Iconic layered canyon, river corridor
destinations.late_october_fit: Arguably the single best late-October trip — summer furnace gone, inner canyon in the comfortable 60s-70s.
pros: Bucket-list, world-famous descent
Ideal late-October temperatures in the inner canyon
Reliable water and developed corridor campground
cons: Corridor permits are competitive — not guaranteed
Brutal climb back out (huge elevation gain)
Requires advance application plus a backup plan
destinations.tradeoff: The best scenery-to-effort payoff on the list, but the permit is a gamble you must work for.

destinations.name: Zion National Park (Narrows / West Rim)
destinations.bookability_note: Wilderness permits via Zion's calendar + seasonal lottery; the Narrows top-down also has last-minute drawings 2-7 days before. A full calendar isn't a dead end.
destinations.best_route: Narrows top-down (~16 mi, one overnight) OR the drier West Rim Trail
destinations.terrain: Slot-canyon river hike (Narrows) or high rim with big views (West Rim)
destinations.late_october_fit: Golden cottonwoods, thinning crowds; flash-flood risk lower than monsoon season but never zero.
pros: One of the most famous canyon hikes anywhere
Fall color along the Virgin River
Last-minute drawings give a second chance at permits
cons: Narrows water is cold — drysuit weather by late October
Must check flash-flood forecast; trips can be weather-canceled
Permits contested; shuttle logistics for the top-down start
destinations.tradeoff: Unforgettable if conditions and permits align, but the most weather-dependent, gear-intensive pick.

destinations.name: Paria Canyon / Buckskin Gulch
destinations.bookability_note: Daily online quota on recreation.gov; far less competitive than spring, generally available now for fall.
destinations.best_route: Wire Pass / Buckskin Gulch into Paria Canyon (overnight or thru-hike to Lee's Ferry)
destinations.terrain: World-class slot canyon and deep river canyon
destinations.late_october_fit: Excellent fall conditions in one of the longest slot canyons on earth.
pros: Genuinely available to book now
Spectacular, immersive slot-canyon experience
Flexible from an overnight to a multi-day thru-hike
cons: Flash-flood danger — forecast checks are non-negotiable
Cold water/mud wades that numb in late season
Car shuttle needed for the full thru-hike
destinations.tradeoff: A bookable, jaw-dropping slot canyon, but demands flash-flood vigilance and slot-canyon competence.

destinations.name: Canyonlands National Park (Needles District)
destinations.bookability_note: Backcountry permits via recreation.gov; fall is prime and popular, so book soon but availability is normal.
destinations.best_route: Chesler Park / Joint Trail loop (backcountry zones)
destinations.terrain: Sandstone spires, slickrock, desert canyons
destinations.late_october_fit: Peak desert backpacking season — warm days, crisp nights, stable weather.
pros: Surreal needle-and-fin rock landscape
Reliable fall weather and bookable permits
Excellent loop options of varied length
cons: Water is scarce — caching or heavy carry required
Popular zones can fill; book ahead
Slickrock route-finding in spots
destinations.tradeoff: Otherworldly scenery you can reserve now, with water logistics as the main planning cost.

destinations.name: Capitol Reef National Park (Backcountry)
destinations.bookability_note: Free backcountry permits, self-registered; rarely any quota pressure.
destinations.best_route: Upper Muley Twist Canyon or Halls Creek Narrows
destinations.terrain: Waterpocket Fold canyons, slickrock, narrows
destinations.late_october_fit: Quiet, cool, and uncrowded — one of the least-trafficked Utah parks in fall.
pros: Free, low-hassle permits
Genuine solitude vs. the big-name parks
Striking, varied canyon terrain
cons: Very limited water; flash-flood awareness needed in narrows
Remote trailheads on dirt roads
Navigation and self-sufficiency required
destinations.tradeoff: Solitude and free permits, but you trade developed infrastructure and easy water.

destinations.name: Coyote Gulch / Grand Staircase-Escalante
destinations.bookability_note: Free self-issue permits at trailhead registers; no quota for most of the area.
destinations.best_route: Hurricane Wash to Coyote Gulch (arches, natural bridge, Escalante River)
destinations.terrain: Lush slot/river canyon with arches and waterfalls
destinations.late_october_fit: Cottonwoods turning gold, mild temps, low water levels for easy wading.
pros: No reservation needed — go anytime
Iconic, photogenic canyon with reliable water
Beginner-friendly canyon backpacking
cons: Long sandy approach and steep exit options
Flash-flood risk during storms
Increasingly popular for an 'off the radar' spot
destinations.tradeoff: One of the easiest world-class canyon trips to just show up and do, but expect company.

destinations.name: Bryce Canyon National Park (Under-the-Rim Trail)
destinations.bookability_note: Backcountry permits at the visitor center; rarely full. NOTE: high elevation makes late October a snow gamble.
destinations.best_route: Under-the-Rim Trail (sections or full ~23 mi)
destinations.terrain: Hoodoo amphitheaters, pine forest, high plateau
destinations.late_october_fit: Stunning when clear, but at ~8,000-9,000 ft it can see hard freezes and early snow.
pros: Unique hoodoo scenery few people backpack through
Permits almost always available
Deep solitude below the rim
cons: Real early-snow and hard-freeze risk in late October
Cold nights demand serious gear
Water sources can be unreliable/dry
destinations.tradeoff: Singular scenery and easy permits, but the highest weather risk on this list — watch the forecast hard.

destinations.name: Aravaipa Canyon Wilderness
destinations.bookability_note: Strict daily quota (50 people) via recreation.gov; fall sometimes has openings but weekends go fast — check now.
destinations.best_route: Aravaipa Canyon (perennial creek walk, east or west entrance)
destinations.terrain: Lush perennial desert stream canyon, cottonwoods, side canyons
destinations.late_october_fit: Comfortable water temps, fall color in the cottonwoods, fewer permits claimed than spring.
pros: Rare year-round desert stream — constant water and shade
Excellent wildlife (bighorn, birds)
Quota keeps it quiet and pristine
cons: Tight permit quota — weekends competitive
Endless creek crossings; wet feet all day
Remote access roads
destinations.tradeoff: A lush desert oasis if you snag a permit, but the quota is the catch.

destinations.name: Superstition Wilderness
destinations.bookability_note: Tonto National Forest — no backpacking permit; dispersed camping. Free campfire permit for stoves/fires.
destinations.best_route: Peralta to Fremont Saddle / Dutchman's Trail loop
destinations.terrain: Saguaro desert, rugged volcanic ridges, canyons
destinations.late_october_fit: Cooler fall temps make this classic Sonoran range comfortable after a brutal summer.
pros: No reservation needed
Quintessential saguaro-desert scenery
Flexible loops of varied length
cons: Water sources unreliable — plan and carry heavy
Daytime can still be warm
Spiny terrain; off-trail navigation in spots
destinations.tradeoff: Permit-free classic desert backpacking, but water scarcity is the constant constraint.

destinations.name: Saguaro National Park (Rincon Backcountry)
destinations.bookability_note: Backcountry permits via recreation.gov; generally available for fall.
destinations.best_route: Douglas Spring / Manning Camp loop into the Rincon Mountains
destinations.terrain: Saguaro desert rising into pine-clad sky-island peaks
destinations.late_october_fit: Desert floor is pleasant and the high country is crisp but not yet snowbound.
pros: Climb from cactus desert to pine forest in one trip
Bookable permits and designated camps with some water
Quiet backcountry beside a popular day-use park
cons: Significant elevation gain to the high camps
Water planning needed on lower stretches
Cold nights up high
destinations.tradeoff: A full ecological gradient in one hike, at the cost of a steep climb.

destinations.name: Chiricahua Mountains (Coronado National Forest)
destinations.bookability_note: National forest wilderness — no permit for backpacking; dispersed camping.
destinations.best_route: Chiricahua Wilderness loops from Rustler Park / Snowshed
destinations.terrain: Sky-island pine forest, rhyolite pinnacles, canyons
destinations.late_october_fit: Sky-island elevation is crisp and colorful in fall; lower trails stay mild.
pros: No permit; remote and uncrowded
Exceptional biodiversity and birding
Dramatic rock formations
cons: Very remote corner of Arizona
Some areas affected by past wildfire
Water sources seasonal/unreliable
destinations.tradeoff: True solitude and unique terrain, but far from anything and fire-scarred in places.

destinations.name: Gila Wilderness (Gila River Route)
destinations.bookability_note: No permit required for backpacking in the wilderness.
destinations.best_route: Gila River canyon from the Gila Cliff Dwellings area
destinations.terrain: River canyon, hot springs, ponderosa forest
destinations.late_october_fit: Lower river routes are mild and colorful; the nation's first wilderness, largely to yourself.
pros: No permit and enormous, wild terrain
Riverside hot springs and reliable water on river routes
Profound solitude
cons: Many river crossings; high water possible after rain
Remote; long drive to trailheads
Higher side-routes can be cold/snowy
destinations.tradeoff: Big, free, lonely wilderness, but river crossings and remoteness raise the commitment.

destinations.name: Big Bend National Park
destinations.bookability_note: Backcountry permits on recreation.gov up to 6 months out; fall availability is usually good.
destinations.best_route: South Rim loop in the Chisos Mountains (~12-14 miles)
destinations.terrain: Desert basin rising into a forested mountain sky island
destinations.late_october_fit: Backpacking season effectively STARTS now — summer is dangerously hot.
pros: Permits readily available for late October
Dramatic sky-island views into Mexico
Deep solitude — one of the least-visited big parks
cons: Extremely remote location and limited services
Water caching/planning needed on some routes
Long drives between trailheads
destinations.tradeoff: Easiest marquee permit to grab, but the remoteness is a real commitment.

destinations.name: Guadalupe Mountains National Park
destinations.bookability_note: Wilderness permits via the park's reservation system; generally available, though designated sites are limited.
destinations.best_route: Tejas / Bowl loop through the high country; McKittrick Canyon for fall color
destinations.terrain: Desert-to-conifer mountains; maple-lined canyon
destinations.late_october_fit: One of the few Southwest spots with real fall foliage — McKittrick Canyon maples peak late Oct/early Nov.
pros: Genuine autumn color in the desert Southwest
Highest peak in Texas plus dramatic ridges
Bookable and uncrowded
cons: Notoriously fierce, sustained winds
Dry camps — carry all water to the high sites
Steep, demanding climbs
destinations.tradeoff: Desert foliage and big views, but wind and waterless camps test you.

destinations.name: Joshua Tree National Park
destinations.bookability_note: No advance backcountry permit; free self-registration at backcountry boards.
destinations.best_route: Boy Scout Trail / Willow Hole or a California Riding & Hiking Trail section
destinations.terrain: High desert, granite boulders, Joshua tree forest
destinations.late_october_fit: Prime season — summer heat broken, cool nights, excellent stargazing.
pros: Zero booking friction — go any weekend
Surreal scenery and world-class night skies
Beginner-friendly terrain and navigation
cons: Must pack in ALL water — no reliable sources
Cold nights (can dip below freezing)
Day-use crowds near trailheads (backcountry thins fast)
destinations.tradeoff: Maximum flexibility and ease, but you carry every drop of water.

destinations.name: Death Valley National Park
destinations.bookability_note: No permit required for backcountry backpacking; free voluntary registration.
destinations.best_route: Cottonwood–Marble Canyons loop
destinations.terrain: Canyon country, dunes, salt flats, vast open desert
destinations.late_october_fit: Flips into its pleasant season — summer is lethal, late October is hikeable.
pros: No permit hassle and total solitude
Otherworldly, varied geology
Incredible dark skies
cons: Water is extremely scarce — heavy carry or careful planning
Remote; long rough-road drives between trailheads
Navigation demands experience
destinations.tradeoff: Epic emptiness for free, but the most logistically demanding desert option on water and access.

destinations.name: Mojave National Preserve
destinations.bookability_note: No permit for backcountry/roadside dispersed camping; very few restrictions.
destinations.best_route: Kelso Dunes area or the Mid Hills / Table Mountain backcountry
destinations.terrain: Sand dunes, Joshua tree forest, volcanic cinder cones, mesas
destinations.late_october_fit: Mild, empty, and ideal once the summer heat fades.
pros: No permit and almost no crowds
Diverse terrain in one preserve
Outstanding dark skies
cons: No water — carry everything
Faint routes; strong navigation needed
Remote with minimal services
destinations.tradeoff: Vast free desert solitude, but you supply all water and routefinding.

destinations.name: Anza-Borrego Desert State Park
destinations.bookability_note: Open dispersed backcountry camping with few restrictions; no reservation for backcountry.
destinations.best_route: Coyote Canyon or the slot canyons / Borrego Palm Canyon area
destinations.terrain: Badlands, palm oases, slot canyons, open desert
destinations.late_october_fit: Cooling into its prime season; palm oases offer shade and occasional water.
pros: Free, flexible dispersed camping
Palm oases, slot canyons, and badlands variety
Largest state park in California — room to roam
cons: Water generally must be carried
Can still be hot early in the season
Sandy/4WD access to some areas
destinations.tradeoff: Easy-access desert freedom, but it's hit-or-miss on early-season heat and water.

destinations.name: Trans-Catalina Trail (Catalina Island)
destinations.bookability_note: Reserve campsites via Catalina Island Conservancy + a free hiking permit; book the Catalina Express ferry. Fall has good availability.
destinations.best_route: Trans-Catalina Trail (Avalon to Two Harbors), ~38.5 miles or shorter sections
destinations.terrain: Coastal ridgeline, coves, bison-dotted grasslands
destinations.late_october_fit: Mild island climate year-round; fall is warm, clear, and uncrowded.
pros: Reliably bookable with designated sites and water
Ocean views the whole way; mild temps, no freezing nights
Free-roaming bison are a novelty
cons: Ferry logistics and schedule to coordinate
Lots of elevation gain for the mileage
Campsites can be exposed/windy
destinations.tradeoff: Easy to book and mild, but you trade true wilderness and pay for ferries.

destinations.name: Channel Islands National Park (Santa Cruz / Santa Rosa)
destinations.bookability_note: Reserve campsites on recreation.gov + book the Island Packers ferry. Rarely sells out for fall.
destinations.best_route: Santa Cruz Island (Scorpion to Smugglers / Potato Harbor) or backcountry Santa Rosa
destinations.terrain: Sea-cliff coastline, island interior, endemic wildlife
destinations.late_october_fit: Mild year-round; fall is calm and quiet with great wildlife viewing.
pros: Remote island feel with minimal crowds
Endemic foxes, sea caves, kayaking/snorkeling add-ons
Mild temperatures
cons: Must pack in all food; limited water depending on island/site
Ferry-dependent — weather can disrupt crossings
Windy, exposed campsites and a small trail network
destinations.tradeoff: A true island adventure that's easy to reserve, but small trail system and ferry weather risk.

destinations.name: Point Reyes National Seashore
destinations.bookability_note: Backcountry campsites reservable on recreation.gov; fall midweek is wide open.
destinations.best_route: Coast Camp / Wildcat Camp via the Coast Trail
destinations.terrain: Coastal bluffs, beaches, grasslands, forest
destinations.late_october_fit: Mild maritime climate; fall is often the clearest, calmest season here.
pros: Reliable reservations and gentle terrain
Beachfront camps and dramatic coastline
Great for less-experienced backpackers
cons: Fog can roll in and obscure views
Short mileage between camps (less remote feel)
Popular weekends still book up
destinations.tradeoff: Easy, scenic, low-commitment coastal backpacking, but not a big-wilderness experience.

destinations.name: Lost Coast Trail (King Range)
destinations.bookability_note: Daily quota permit on recreation.gov; late October is shoulder season, so generally available now.
destinations.best_route: Mattole to Black Sands Beach (~25 miles, northbound)
destinations.terrain: Black-sand beaches, sea bluffs, no-road wilderness coastline
destinations.late_october_fit: Low elevation stays hikeable when mountains close out; fewer crowds than summer.
pros: Bookable now for a marquee, bucket-list coastal route
Stunning, roadless wilderness coastline
Reliable water from creeks crossing the trail
cons: Tide tables are mandatory — some stretches impassable at high tide
Soft sand and round rock make tiring miles
Fall weather can bring rain/fog; shuttle logistics required
destinations.tradeoff: A world-class trip you can still book, but tide-dependent and weather-exposed.

destinations.name: Sespe Wilderness / Los Padres National Forest
destinations.bookability_note: No backcountry permit for backpacking; free California Campfire Permit for stoves/fires, Adventure Pass at some trailheads. CHECK current fire/seasonal closures.
destinations.best_route: Piedra Blanca / Sespe River Trail toward Sespe Hot Springs
destinations.terrain: Chaparral, river canyon, sandstone formations, hot springs
destinations.late_october_fit: Cooler fall temps make the exposed, low-water canyon comfortable; payoff is wild hot springs.
pros: No reservation needed
Natural hot springs as a destination
Solitude and a true local-wilderness feel
cons: Fire closures are common in Los Padres — verify before you go
Limited/seasonal water; condor-sanctuary closures in spots
Faint trails and long approach to the springs
destinations.tradeoff: Permit-free trip to wild hot springs, but the most closure-prone and least certain to be open.

destinations.name: Olympic National Park (Wilderness Coast)
destinations.bookability_note: Wilderness camping permits via recreation.gov; bear canister required. Bookable, but expect rain.
destinations.best_route: Ozette Triangle or the South Coast (Third Beach to Oil City)
destinations.terrain: Wild Pacific beaches, sea stacks, rainforest fringe
destinations.late_october_fit: Low-elevation coast stays hikeable while the mountains shut down — but it's the wet season.
pros: Dramatic, remote wilderness coastline and sea stacks
Permits generally available in the off-season
Abundant water and tide-pool wildlife
cons: Frequent heavy rain in late October
Tide tables mandatory for headland crossings
Slippery, rugged terrain; bear canister required
destinations.tradeoff: Spectacular and bookable, but you're betting against Pacific Northwest rain.

destinations.name: Great Smoky Mountains National Park
destinations.bookability_note: Backcountry permits/reservations via the park's smokiespermits system; generally bookable now, though popular shelters can fill.
destinations.best_route: Appalachian Trail section or a Deep Creek / Hazel Creek loop
destinations.terrain: Forested ridgelines, streams, peak foliage at lower elevations
destinations.late_october_fit: Lower elevations hit peak fall color in late October — the classic foliage trip.
pros: Spectacular autumn color and abundant water
Large trail network with many loop options
Permits generally available
cons: Cold, wet weather possible; early snow at high elevations
Black bear country — strict food storage
Popular shelters/sites can book up
destinations.tradeoff: Premier foliage backpacking, but variable mountain weather and bear protocols.

destinations.name: Shenandoah National Park
destinations.bookability_note: Free backcountry permit, self-registered; no quota for most areas.
destinations.best_route: Appalachian Trail / Old Rag and Whiteoak Canyon loops off Skyline Drive
destinations.terrain: Blue Ridge forest, waterfalls, ridgeline views
destinations.late_october_fit: Foliage peaks mid-to-late October along the Blue Ridge; easy free permits.
pros: Free, low-hassle permits and easy access
Beautiful fall color and waterfalls
Plentiful water and well-marked trails
cons: Foliage-season crowds on Skyline Drive corridor
Cold snaps and possible early frost
Bear country — proper food storage required
destinations.tradeoff: Among the easiest foliage backpacking to just go do, but the corridor draws leaf-peeper crowds.

destinations.name: Dolly Sods Wilderness
destinations.bookability_note: Monongahela National Forest wilderness — no permit; dispersed camping.
destinations.best_route: Dolly Sods loop (Rohrbaugh / Red Creek trails)
destinations.terrain: High-plateau bogs, heath barrens, red spruce, open vistas
destinations.late_october_fit: Striking tundra-like fall color, but the high plateau gets cold and exposed.
pros: Unique, otherworldly high-bog landscape
No permit and genuine solitude
Vivid autumn color in the heath
cons: Cold, wind, and possible early snow on the exposed plateau
Boggy, wet trails and frequent water crossings
Navigation can be tricky in fog
destinations.tradeoff: A one-of-a-kind landscape for free, but the weather exposure is the price.

destinations.name: Cumberland Island National Seashore
destinations.bookability_note: Campsite + ferry reservations via recreation.gov and the park ferry; fall is comfortable and bookable.
destinations.best_route: Sea Camp to Hickory Hill / Stafford backcountry sites
destinations.terrain: Maritime forest, wild beaches, salt marsh, live oaks
destinations.late_october_fit: Summer heat and bugs ease; mild, atmospheric coastal backpacking with wild horses.
pros: Mild fall weather and easy terrain
Wild horses, beaches, and Spanish-moss forest
Reservable sites and ferry
cons: Ferry-dependent with limited daily capacity
Must pack in everything; treat/carry water
Lingering bugs and humidity early in the season
destinations.tradeoff: Uniquely atmospheric coastal trip that's easy to book, but ferry logistics gate access.

destinations.name: Ozark Highlands Trail / Buffalo River Area
destinations.bookability_note: National forest / national river — no permit for most backpacking; dispersed camping.
destinations.best_route: Ozark Highlands Trail sections or the Buffalo River Trail
destinations.terrain: Hardwood forest, bluffs, waterfalls, river valleys
destinations.late_october_fit: Peak Ozark fall color with mild days and crisp nights.
pros: Excellent, underrated autumn foliage
No permit and abundant water
Bluff-top views and waterfalls
cons: Trail can be overgrown or faint in sections
Frequent creek crossings (high after rain)
Ticks/chiggers can linger in warm spells
destinations.tradeoff: Quiet, beautiful foliage backpacking for free, but trail conditions are rougher than marquee parks.

destinations.name: Everglades National Park (Backcountry)
destinations.bookability_note: Wilderness/backcountry permits via recreation.gov. NOTE: late October is the tail of the wet season — better in Nov-Dec.
destinations.best_route: Coastal chickee/ground sites along the Wilderness Waterway or Cape Sable (often paddle-access)
destinations.difficulty: Easy on foot, but logistically complex; many sites are water-access
destinations.terrain: Mangrove coast, sawgrass, chickee platforms, beaches
destinations.late_october_fit: A warm-weather option when everywhere else is cooling, but still hot, humid, and buggy this early.
pros: Tropical, totally different from the rest of the list
Bookable permits and unique paddle-and-camp routes
Abundant wildlife
cons: Heavy mosquitoes and humidity in late October
Many sites require a canoe/kayak, not just hiking
Lingering hurricane-season weather risk
destinations.tradeoff: The warmest, most exotic pick, but late October is too early for its prime — expect bugs and heat.

ruled_out.name: Havasupai (Havasu Falls)
ruled_out.reason: For 2026 the Havasupai Tribe discontinued both the lottery and the transfer board. Permits for desirable dates (including late October) sell out within minutes of release, and the only remaining last-minute path is randomly re-released cancellations on the official booking page — too unreliable to plan around. Late October is also a historically flash-flood-prone window here.
ruled_out.note: Worth flagging in the artifact as 'considered but not viable last-minute' so others don't waste time chasing it.

general_tips: Daylight is ~11 hours in late October — plan shorter trail days.
Pack a warm sleeping bag; desert and canyon nights can hit or drop below freezing, and high-elevation entries can see snow.
For any canyon or river route (Grand Canyon, Narrows, Paria, Coyote Gulch, Aravaipa), pull a fresh flash-flood forecast before committing.
For coastal routes (Lost Coast, Olympic, Channel Islands), check tide tables and ferry schedules.

Open-access options (Joshua Tree, Death Valley, Mojave, Anza-Borrego, Superstition, Chiricahua, Gila, Capitol Reef, Coyote Gulch, Sespe, Shenandoah, Dolly Sods, Ozarks) are the safest bets if you truly want to decide last-minute.
Confirm current permit rules, quotas, and fire/seasonal closures on the official site for each destination before you go.
